cluster.title: Saving & Spending Calculators — Kefiw
cluster.subhead: Discounts, tips, margins, break-even — the money-light tools.
cluster.intro: Calculators for small decisions: discount stacking, tip splits, markup vs margin, break-even. This cluster bundles them with short explainers for the traps (like stacked discounts compounding, or margin vs markup being different).
cluster.description: Discount calculator, tip calculator, markup/margin calculator, and break-even calculator, with short guides to stacked discounts, tip etiquette, and margin math.

faq.a: No — two 20%-off coupons give 36% off, not 40%. Each discount applies to the already-reduced price.

faq.q: Is markup the same as margin?
faq.a: No. Markup divides profit by cost; margin divides profit by price. Same profit, different denominator — so margin is always smaller.

tools.title: Horizon Point — Buy vs Rent Break-Even

current.description: The exact year buying a home becomes cheaper than renting. Dual-line cumulative cost chart.
current.intro: Monthly rent, home price, down payment, mortgage rate, maintenance, and appreciation feed a 30-year simulation. The chart overlays cumulative rent against net-of-equity ownership cost; the Horizon Point marks the break-even year.
current.outcomeLine: The exact year buying a home overtakes renting on cumulative cost.
howTo: Set Monthly Rent and Home Price.
Adjust Down Payment %, Interest Rate, Maintenance % of price/year, and expected Appreciation.
Ownership includes interest, property tax (~1.25%/yr), maintenance, down payment, and 3% closing.
The Horizon Point is where cumulative rent equals ownership cost minus equity built.

examples.title: $2,200 rent, $450k home, 20% down, 6.5%
examples.body: Break-even near year 6–8 depending on appreciation and maintenance assumptions.

examples.title: No appreciation
examples.body: Break-even slides out several years; in hot-rent markets it may still arrive before year 10.

faq.q: Why subtract equity from ownership cost?
faq.a: Equity is real value you keep when selling — it offsets the cash out. Renting has no equivalent store of value.

faq.q: What if the horizon never arrives?
faq.a: The verdict shows No Break-Even. At those inputs (often low appreciation + high rate), renting stays cheaper across the 30-year window.
Yes — ownership starts with a 3% closing cost added to the down payment at month 0.

tools.title: Runway Zero — Cash Runway to Bankruptcy

current.description: Calculate the exact month your cash runs out. Crisis toggle models a worst-case scenario with revenue zeroed.
current.intro: Enter cash on hand, monthly burn, and monthly revenue. The calculator projects cash balance month-by-month and pinpoints the exact Zero Date. Toggle Crisis mode to zero out revenue and model the worst-case shock.
current.outcomeLine: Exact month your cash hits zero — with a Crisis toggle that zeros revenue.
howTo: Enter your current Cash Balance.
Enter your total Monthly Burn (all outflows).
Enter your Monthly Revenue (all inflows).
Runway = Cash ÷ (Burn − Revenue). Enable Crisis to reduce revenue to $0.

examples.title: $250k cash, $65k burn, $22k revenue
examples.body: Net burn $43k/month → ~5.8 months runway. Zero date roughly 6 months out.

examples.title: Crisis mode on same inputs
examples.body: Revenue drops to $0 → runway shortens to ~3.8 months (250k ÷ 65k).

faq.a: Every outflow: salaries, rent, infrastructure, marketing, taxes, contractors. If it leaves the bank each month, include it.
The calculator shows an Indefinite status. No runway zero exists at current pace.
Yes — all inputs persist to localStorage on this device and nowhere else.

tools.title: Shock Survival — Loan Buffer After an Emergency

current.description: How many months of debt service survive an unexpected shock expense. Critical warning under 3 months.
current.intro: Given your savings, a recurring loan payment, and a one-time shock expense, the Survival Window shows how many months of debt service remain. A Survival Gauge tracks 0–12+ months with a critical warning state under 3.
current.outcomeLine: Months of fixed debt service that survive a one-time shock expense.
howTo: Enter your total liquid Savings.
Enter your recurring Monthly Loan Payment.
Enter the Shock Expense (the one-time event you're stress-testing).
Survival = (Savings − Shock) ÷ Monthly Payment.

faq.q: Why 3 months as the critical threshold?
faq.a: Under 3 months of debt service buffer is the conventional high-default-risk band. The gauge turns red and flags the warning state.
No — this is a pure buffer model. Assume the shock also cut off income; if income continues, add it to savings.

tools.title: The Crossover — Subscription vs Lifetime Purchase

current.description: The exact month when a monthly subscription overtakes a one-time lifetime purchase. Opportunity-cost toggle included.
current.intro: Compare a recurring subscription against a one-time "lifetime" price. The crossover month is when cumulative subscription cost overtakes the lifetime price. Toggle Opportunity Cost to compound the lifetime sum at a chosen annual return.
current.outcomeLine: The month a monthly subscription becomes more expensive than the lifetime buy.
howTo: Enter the Monthly Subscription fee and the one-time Lifetime Price.
Crossover = Lifetime ÷ Monthly.
Enable Opportunity Cost to model the lifetime sum invested at (default 5%) annually — the crossover shifts out.
Read the Verdict: "Buy if using for more than X months."

examples.title: $12/mo vs $299 lifetime
examples.body: Crossover at ~25 months. Buy if you'll use it longer than ~2 years.

examples.title: Same inputs + 5% opportunity cost
examples.body: Crossover shifts to ~27 months — the uninvested $299 would have grown meanwhile.

faq.a: A lifetime purchase ties up cash that could have been invested. Modeling the alternative return pushes the crossover out.

faq.q: When should I favor the subscription?
faq.a: If usage is uncertain, the software evolves quickly, or you'd use it for fewer months than the crossover.

tools.title: Minimum Viable Rate — Freelancer Reality Check

current.description: The absolute minimum hourly rate to match a corporate salary after self-employment tax, benefits, and non-billable time.
current.intro: Annual need (salary + benefits + overhead) divided by effective billable hours (weeks × hours × utilization). Utilization is the hidden lever — at 60%, 40 weekly hours becomes 24 actually billable.
current.outcomeLine: Minimum freelance hourly to match a corporate salary after invisible costs.
howTo: Enter Target Salary, Benefits Value, Business Overhead (all annual).
Enter Billable Weeks and Hours/Week.
Set Utilization % — the share of your time that is actually invoiced.
MVR = (salary + benefits + overhead) ÷ effective hours.

examples.title: $120k + $18k benefits + $9k overhead, 47w × 40h × 60%
examples.body: MVR ≈ $130/hr. Anything less is a pay cut vs the W-2 equivalent.

faq.a: 50–70% is typical. New freelancers underestimate admin/sales time; veterans with a pipeline hit 75%+.

tools.title: Gig Net Floor — Real Hourly After Mileage Cost

current.description: What DoorDash or Uber actually pays after IRS mileage depreciation — real hourly vs the minimum wage floor.
current.intro: Operating cost = miles × $0.67 (2024 IRS standard rate — captures gas + maintenance + depreciation, not just gas). Real hourly = (gross − operating cost) ÷ hours. If real hourly is below federal or local minimum wage, you are trading car equity for app cash.
current.outcomeLine: Your real hourly after IRS mileage cost — minimum wage sanity check.
howTo: Enter Gross Pay for the shift, Miles Driven, and Hours Worked.
Set Gas Price and Local Minimum Wage for sanity checks.
The dial shows real hourly vs the minimum wage floor.

examples.title: $140 / 95mi / 6hr shift
examples.body: Operating cost $63.65 → real profit $76.35 → $12.73/hr. Above federal min, below $15 local min.

faq.q: Why IRS $0.67 and not just gas?
faq.a: Because your tires, oil, brakes, and the car itself all wear per mile. The IRS rate is the closest thing to a true per-mile cost.

tools.title: Dopamine Minimum — The One Task Pivot

current.description: Pick the ONE task with the best visibility-to-effort ratio when a full to-do list is too much to face.
current.intro: Momentum = visibility ÷ effort. Rank your tasks and the single highest-momentum one gets the glowing button. You do one thing. The rest waits. The compounded effect of one small visible win is usually enough to pivot the day.
current.outcomeLine: The ONE task with the best visibility-to-effort ratio when a full list feels impossible.
howTo: List 3-10 candidate tasks.
Rate visibility (1-10) — how obviously done does it look?
Rate effort (1-10) — how much does it actually take?
The top of the rank is your one task. Do only that one.

examples.title: Made bed beats deep clean

articles.title: Buy vs Rent Break-Even — When Does Ownership Win?
articles.subhead: Cumulative ownership cost minus equity, compared to cumulative rent.

current.description: The buy-vs-rent break-even model — what goes in, what the horizon point means, and how appreciation and maintenance move the year.
current.outcomeLine: Horizon point = first month where cumulative rent ≥ cumulative ownership cost minus equity built. Inputs: rate, appreciation, maintenance.
current.intro: Buying a home is only cheaper than renting after enough years. The horizon point is that year — where cumulative rent overtakes cumulative ownership cost net of equity built.
keyPoints: Monthly ownership cost = mortgage payment + property tax + maintenance. Equity grows each month as principal is paid.
Net ownership cost = cumulative ownership cost − equity built − home appreciation.
Rent cost compounds annually at a typical 2–4% escalator. Model the escalator honestly — ignoring it flatters renting.
Interest rate dominates the first decade; appreciation dominates the second. A 1% rate difference can shift the horizon by 3–5 years.
If the horizon never arrives inside the 30-year term, the inputs favor renting — usually high rate + low appreciation.

examples.title: Typical case
examples.body: $400k home, 20% down, 6.5% rate, $2.2k rent, 3% appreciation, 1% maintenance → horizon around year 8.

examples.title: High-cost market
examples.body: Same home, rent $1.8k, 2% appreciation → horizon pushes past year 14. Renting wins unless you stay long.

examples.title: Rate-sensitive
examples.body: Drop rate to 5%, keep everything else → horizon shrinks to year 6. Rate moves the line hard.

whenToUse.note: Main tool — sliders for rate, appreciation, maintenance, down payment. Shows the exact horizon year.
For the pure monthly payment before combining with rent.
For modeling how prepayment shifts the horizon earlier.

faq.q: Why does appreciation get added to equity?
faq.a: Because selling realizes both the principal paid down AND the market gain. The net-ownership line reflects what you would net if you sold at that month.
No — model inputs are pre-tax. Mortgage interest deductions narrow the horizon by months, not years; the tool ignores them for simplicity.

articles.title: Cash Runway Basics — How Long Until Zero?
articles.subhead: Burn rate, net burn, and the exact month the bank account hits zero.

current.description: Plain-English runway math — how burn, revenue, and net burn combine into the exact month the cash runs out.
current.outcomeLine: Runway months = cash ÷ net burn. Net burn = burn − revenue. A crisis toggle zeroes revenue to stress-test.
current.intro: Runway is the number of months you can keep operating before the cash balance hits zero. The formula is one division — but the inputs and the stress test are what matter.
keyPoints: Gross burn: total monthly outgoing — payroll, rent, hosting, everything.
Net burn = gross burn − revenue. Negative net burn means you are profitable (no runway zero).
Runway months = current cash ÷ net burn. If net burn is zero or negative, runway is indefinite.
Burn multiple = net burn ÷ net new revenue. Under 2× is healthy; over 4× signals inefficient growth.
The Crisis toggle models a revenue-collapse scenario by zeroing income and recomputing runway against gross burn alone.

examples.title: Healthy SaaS
examples.body: Cash $1.2M, burn $120k/mo, revenue $40k/mo. Net burn $80k → 15 months runway.

examples.title: Crisis scenario
examples.body: Same company, revenue cut to zero. Runway collapses to $1.2M ÷ $120k = 10 months. The 5-month gap is your revenue dependency.

examples.title: Approaching profitability
examples.body: Revenue $110k vs burn $120k → net burn $10k, runway 120 months. Close enough to call "indefinite" in planning terms.

whenToUse.note: Main tool — enter cash, burn, and revenue; get the exact zero date.
For a single shock-event buffer (not ongoing burn).
For the inverse — how long to hit a target balance at a given save rate.

faq.q: Should I include a funding round I expect to close?
faq.a: No — runway is what you have now. Model the round as a separate scenario. Counting expected capital as runway is how companies run out of cash.
Use the trailing 3-month average for a smoothed signal, or the worst recent month for a conservative read. Annualizing a single spike overstates runway risk.

articles.title: Shock Buffer Basics — How Many Months After an Emergency?
articles.subhead: Debt-service survival math: savings minus shock, divided by monthly payment.

current.description: How to size an emergency buffer against fixed debt service — the formula, the bands, and the traps when income stops with the shock.
current.outcomeLine: Survival months = (savings − shock) ÷ monthly payment. Under 3 months is the red band; 6+ months is stable.
current.intro: A shock buffer answers one question: if an unexpected expense hits and income pauses, how many months of fixed debt service survive? Three numbers, one subtraction, one division.
keyPoints: Subtract the shock from savings first — the buffer is what remains after the emergency lands.
Divide remaining savings by monthly debt service (rent or mortgage + required loan payments). Skip discretionary spending.
Under 3 months is the conventional high-default-risk band — lenders and mortgage brokers flag this.
3–6 months is guarded. 6–12 months is stable. 12+ is fortified.
Income continuing through the shock shifts the math — treat as a reduced shock rather than adding back to savings.

examples.title: Car repair hit
examples.body: Savings $15k, shock $3k, debt service $2.1k/mo. (15k − 3k) ÷ 2.1k = 5.7 months → guarded.

examples.title: Medical + job loss
examples.body: Savings $15k, shock $8k (medical + deductible), debt service $2.1k/mo → 3.3 months. Just above critical.

examples.title: Fortified
examples.body: Savings $40k, same shock, same payment → 17.6 months. Enough runway to reprice the whole situation.

whenToUse.note: Main tool — enter savings, monthly payment, and the shock expense.
For ongoing burn (a business or household with continuing spend) rather than a one-time shock.
To find the monthly debt-service number for a specific mortgage scenario.

faq.q: Should I count my investments as savings?
faq.a: Count only what is liquid within a week without a loss. Brokerage cash and money-market holdings count; retirement accounts with early-withdrawal penalties do not.

faq.q: What if I have more than one loan?
faq.a: Add all required monthly payments into a single debt-service number. The buffer has to cover the total, not each loan individually.

articles.title: Subscription vs Lifetime Crossover — When Does Monthly Lose?
articles.subhead: Divide the lifetime price by the monthly fee — with an optional opportunity-cost correction.

current.description: The math for deciding between a subscription and a one-time lifetime purchase — including the opportunity-cost correction that changes the answer.
current.outcomeLine: Crossover months = lifetime ÷ monthly. With opportunity cost, the lifetime compounds at your assumed return and the crossover extends.
current.intro: A lifetime purchase only beats a subscription if you use the product long enough to recoup the upfront cost. The crossover is the month that happens — the simple version is one division, the honest version accounts for what that lump sum could earn elsewhere.
keyPoints: Simple crossover = lifetime price ÷ monthly price. Past that month, subscription cost exceeds lifetime.
Opportunity-cost crossover: the lifetime amount compounds at your assumed return; subscription months have to overtake the growing alternative balance.
Typical crossovers land at 30–60 months for software — meaning "buy" is only worth it if you are confident you will use it 3–5 years.
Software that evolves fast (AI tools, dev tools, creative suites) favors subscription — the crossover is longer than the useful life of a specific version.
Stable, mature tools (text editors, utilities, disk tools) favor lifetime — they stay useful past the crossover.

examples.title: Typical app
examples.body: $9.99/mo subscription vs $199 lifetime → crossover at month 20. Buy if you expect >20 months of use.

examples.title: With 5% opportunity cost
examples.body: Same prices, 5% annual return on the $199 → crossover pushes to month 24. Small shift but real.

examples.title: Expensive lifetime
examples.body: $14.99/mo vs $499 lifetime → crossover at month 33. Buy-only-if-you-are-sure territory.

whenToUse.note: Main tool — subscription vs lifetime price, with opportunity-cost toggle.
For the bigger version of the same question — home ownership vs renting.
If you are the seller setting subscription vs one-time pricing.

faq.q: Should I factor in future price increases?
faq.a: Subscriptions usually rise 3–8% annually; lifetime is locked. A rising subscription shortens the crossover — model it by bumping the monthly figure 10–20% above today.

faq.q: What if the lifetime includes a year of updates only?
faq.a: Then it is not really lifetime — it is prepaid annual. Compare on equal terms: total cost of 12 months of the subscription vs the prepaid year.

articles.title: Side-Hustle to Full-Time — When Is It Safe to Leap?
articles.subhead: Net salary + benefits vs hustle revenue minus expenses and a 25% self-employment tax reserve.

current.description: The math behind the quit-your-job decision — why the 1.25× safety buffer exists and why benefits must be valued separately from salary.
current.outcomeLine: Leap when hustle net ≥ total comp × safety buffer. Reserve 25% of hustle revenue for self-employment tax.
current.intro: The leap from side-hustle to full-time has two math traps: self-employment tax (15.3% payroll alone, 25%+ with income tax) and the unpriced value of employer benefits. Getting either wrong turns a seeming break-even into a pay cut.
keyPoints: Total comp = net salary + benefits value. Benefits include health insurance, 401k match, paid time off, equipment, professional development.
Hustle net = (revenue − expenses) × 0.75. The 25% reserves federal income + SE tax.
A 1.25× safety buffer is standard — it covers a bad month without forcing a return to employment.
Compound monthly growth: at 6%/yr hustle growth, it takes ~4 years to double. At 12%/yr, ~2 years.
Never use best-month revenue as the input; use a 12-month trailing average.

examples.body: Salary $6.5k + benefits $800 = $7.3k target. Hustle $2.2k at 6%/yr hits that net around month 36.

examples.title: With safety buffer
examples.body: 1.25× target = $9.1k. Same hustle reaches it around month 52 — over a year of extra runway before leaping.

examples.title: Hustle stagnant
examples.body: If growth is <2%/yr, the leap may never arrive at the current rate. Raise prices or expand before quitting.

For pricing your hustle hourly once you go full-time.
For the post-leap survival check in case growth stalls.

faq.a: Payroll tax alone (SS + Medicare) is 15.3%; add federal income tax at 10–22% and most mid-bracket freelancers owe 25–30% on net earnings. High earners should model 30%+.

articles.title: Hire vs Automate — When Does Each Win?
articles.subhead: Hourly wage times hours vs tool stack plus amortized setup — with a manager overhead tax.

current.description: How to frame a hire-vs-automate decision honestly — what hidden costs to include on each side.
current.outcomeLine: Human cost = wage × hours × (1 + overhead %). Automation cost = tool stack + (setup hours × wage ÷ 12).
current.intro: Hire vs automate is not a philosophical question — it is an arithmetic one with a known formula. The trick is counting the hidden costs on both sides: manager time on the human side, setup hours on the automation side.
keyPoints: Baseline: wage × hours vs tool subscriptions per month.
Human hidden cost: 15–20% manager overhead. Every report pulls time from you for reviews, meetings, rework.
Automation hidden cost: setup hours × your hourly rate, amortized over 12 months. Most tools aren't plug-and-play.
Volume tasks favor automation; judgment tasks favor humans.
Recompute quarterly — tool prices and staff throughput both move.

examples.title: Customer support
examples.body: Volume fits chatbots for tier-1; tier-2 judgment stays human. Most shops run a hybrid — measure cost per ticket type.

examples.title: Content review
examples.body: High judgment, low volume — human beats automation even with overhead, because mistakes have a real cost.

articles.title: What Is Your Time Worth? — The Value Floor
articles.subhead: Your productive hourly rate is the baseline. Outsource when a pro can do it for less.

current.description: How to set your internal hourly rate and use it as the filter for every DIY-vs-outsource decision.
current.outcomeLine: DIY cost = (hourly rate × hours) + parts. If > quote, delegate.
current.intro: Most people under-price their time for "money-saving" tasks. Setting an explicit hourly rate — and actually using it — flips that default. The rate should be what you earn when you are most productive, not the minimum wage.
keyPoints: Use your productive hourly rate: freelance rate, or (salary + benefits) ÷ productive hours.
DIY cost includes hours required + parts. Skill-gap time extends hours — add a 50% buffer if you have never done the task before.
Outsource wins when the pro is specialized and fast; DIY wins when the task is simple or you learn something reusable.
Health, urgency, and safety aren't in the math — add them as a thumb on the scale.
Run the tool monthly for recurring tasks — rates and quotes both drift.

To figure out what your hourly rate actually is.
For the investment version — is this money better spent or invested?

faq.q: What rate should I use if I'm salaried?
faq.a: (Salary + benefits) ÷ (2,000 × productivity factor). Productivity factor of 0.6 for most roles — you don't output 100% of 2,000 hours.

articles.title: Cloud Exit — When Self-Hosting Beats Managed
articles.subhead: Cumulative cloud cost vs (hardware ÷ depreciation) + electricity + maintenance + uptime risk.

current.description: The arithmetic behind running your own rack — what to count on each side and why egress usually tips the verdict fast.
current.outcomeLine: Self-host crossover = month cumulative cloud cost ≥ cumulative self-host cost. Egress is the silent killer.
current.intro: Self-hosting gets mocked until the cloud bill clears $2k/mo — then it is obvious. The question is when, specifically, it flips. The math has four hidden costs on the self-host side and one silent killer on the cloud side.
keyPoints: Self-host monthly = hardware ÷ depreciation months + electricity + (maintenance hours × your rate) + (cloud × uptime risk %).

Uptime risk: treat as a % of cloud spend you "save up for" outages — typical 2–5%.
Data egress is the cloud cost people ignore — sometimes 10–30% of the bill on streaming or ML workloads.
Self-hosting wins on steady workloads; cloud wins on spiky ones where hardware would sit idle.

articles.title: Minimum Viable Rate — What to Actually Charge
articles.subhead: Freelance hourly must cover salary + benefits + overhead ÷ effective billable hours.

current.description: The true minimum hourly a freelancer must charge to match corporate compensation — and why utilization is the lever everyone misses.
current.outcomeLine: MVR = (salary + benefits + overhead) ÷ (weeks × hours/week × utilization %).
current.intro: Most freelancers charge what feels "reasonable" and are shocked when they end up making less than a W-2 equivalent. The math is not a feeling — it is four inputs and a division.
keyPoints: Annual need = salary + benefits + business overhead. All three are real, all three must be recovered in billable hours.
Effective billable hours = weeks × hours/week × utilization. Utilization captures non-billable time: admin, sales, sick days, interviews.

Benefits value: $15–25k/yr for a mid-career professional (health, retirement match, PTO, equipment).
Overhead: tools, accounting, coworking, training. $5–15k/yr typical.

articles.title: Opportunity Cost — The S&P 500 Filter
articles.subhead: Compound growth turns $10k into $38.7k over 20 years at 7%. Your spend must beat that.

current.description: The most unforgiving investment filter — forces every dollar to justify itself against the passive market alternative.
current.outcomeLine: Future value = principal × (1 + return)^years. If FV > 5× principal, the opportunity cost is high.
current.intro: Every dollar spent has an opportunity cost: what it would have grown into if left in the market. The S&P 500 Reality Check turns that implicit cost into an explicit number. If the spend can't beat market growth over the same horizon, it's underperforming.
keyPoints: Default 7%: long-run real (inflation-adjusted) return of the S&P 500. Use for honest comparisons.
10-year rule of thumb: money roughly doubles at 7%. Triples at 12%.
Time dominates rate. 30 years at 5% beats 10 years at 10% in absolute growth.
5× multiple is the "high opportunity cost" band — the spend must have 5× more value to you than the cash alone.
Does not include taxes — in taxable accounts, deduct 15–25% from the future value.

articles.title: Tech Debt Interest — The Compounding Cost of Hacks
articles.subhead: Initial fix × (1 + growth)^months — velocity makes the exponent larger.

current.description: A deterministic model for how expensive a shortcut becomes over time — so you can schedule repayment instead of feeling it.
current.outcomeLine: Payoff hours = initial fix × (1 + growth × (1 + velocity/10))^months. Features built on top compound the drag.
current.intro: Tech debt isn't a vibe — it's a compounding cost function. Every new feature built on top of a hack makes the payoff larger. Quantifying it forces the conversation about when, not whether, to pay it down.
keyPoints: Payoff grows exponentially: 8%/mo compounds to ~3× in 18 months, ~6× in 24.
Code velocity multiplies growth: if 5 features land on the hack per month, effective growth rate is ~1.5× the base.
Heatmap bands: <10hr green (manageable), 10–25hr warn, 25–60hr amber, 60+ red (must pay down before more builds on it).
Not all debt compounds — isolated shortcuts in dead code decay slowly. Touch-frequency is the key signal.
The best time to refactor is before the next feature lands on top.

examples.title: Typical hack
examples.body: 6hr shortcut, 8%/mo growth, velocity 5 → 4×payoff at month 12. Schedule repayment by month 9.

examples.title: High-churn module
examples.body: 6hr shortcut, 12%/mo growth, velocity 8 → 10× payoff at month 15. Don't ship without a plan.

examples.title: Low-churn utility
examples.body: 6hr shortcut, 2%/mo growth, velocity 1 → 1.3× payoff at month 12. Can wait if priorities are elsewhere.

For deciding if a refactor or replacement tool is cheaper than ongoing maintenance.

faq.q: Is this quantitative or just directional?
faq.a: Directional — an order-of-magnitude tool. The point is to force scheduling, not to nail the hour count.

articles.title: Revenue per Head — The Hiring Filter
articles.subhead: If the next hire drops RPH below current levels, you are scaling bloat — not revenue.

current.description: The metric that separates real scaling from empire-building — and how to include management overhead and ramp-up honestly.
current.outcomeLine: Projected RPH = (revenue + net added value) ÷ (employees + 1). Hire only if RPH rises.
current.intro: Revenue per head is the single metric that tells you if your company is scaling up or scaling sideways. A hire that drops RPH is bloat unless load or specialization justifies it — and bloat compounds just like tech debt.
keyPoints: RPH = revenue ÷ employees. Simple; the trap is what you count on each side.
Loaded cost = salary × (1 + management tax %). Management tax: 15–20% of a manager's time per direct report.
Ramp penalty: 50% productivity during the ramp-up window. A 6-month ramp on a $100k hire = $25k lost productivity.
Verdict bands: HIRE (RPH rises), MARGINAL (within 5% below current), BLOAT (>5% below).
Watch the trend across hires, not just the next one. Three marginal hires in a row is bloat accumulating.

articles.title: Calorie per Dollar — Brutal Budget Math
articles.subhead: Calories ÷ price, ranked. Survival-window math, not a nutrition plan.

current.description: The survival-budget arithmetic for hard-capped food spending — and why it is not the same as eating well.
current.outcomeLine: Cal/$ = total calories ÷ price. Highest = most fuel per cent. Use as a floor, not a diet.
current.intro: When the food budget is fixed and small, the question is fuel efficiency: how many calories does each dollar buy? The answer is often counter-intuitive — grains and fats dominate, fast food is near the bottom, greens are expensive per calorie.
keyPoints: Top calorie-per-dollar foods: rice, flour, dried beans, oils, peanut butter. Typically 500–1,500 cal/$.
Bottom: pre-prepared meals, most restaurant food, specialty diet products. Typically 50–200 cal/$.
Produce is expensive per calorie but critical for nutrition — budget separately, don't optimize into a rice-only diet.
Seasonal + bulk purchasing can 2×–5× the cal/$ of any staple.
Calculate per package, not per serving — avoids serving-size manipulation.

examples.title: Fast food combo
examples.body: $12 for ~1,100 cal → 92 cal/$. 13× worse than rice.

whenToUse.note: Main tool — add items and rank by cal/$.
For allocating limited cash between food and other critical bills.
When gig work is funding the food budget.

faq.q: Is rice really all I should eat?
faq.a: No — this is survival math, not nutrition. Use the top of the list as a calorie base; spend extra budget on vegetables, cheap protein, and variety.

faq.q: What about time cost to cook?
faq.a: Dry staples take prep time. Factor it in if time is scarce — eggs, canned beans, and peanut butter beat rice for quick calories.

articles.title: Bill Triage — Which to Pay First When You Can't Pay All
articles.subhead: Score = (essential level × 10) + (late fee ÷ amount × 100). Service cutoff dominates.

current.description: Emergency prioritization rules when the mailbox stack exceeds the bank account — what the math actually says.
current.outcomeLine: Pay first: water/electric > car/rent > internet > credit card. Bump credit card higher only if the fee ratio is brutal.
current.intro: Decision fatigue kills judgment in a cash crisis. A weighted score — essentiality plus relative late-fee pain — pulls emotion out of the mailbox stack and tells you what to pay when.
keyPoints: Essential levels: 4 water/electric (service cutoff = unsafe), 3 car/rent (transportation + shelter), 2 internet (income-critical for most), 1 credit card (credit hit but no service loss).

Service cutoff bills move first — the reconnection fee is often 3–5× the missed payment.
Small bills with high late-fee ratios can jump the list — $25 late on a $60 bill is 42 score points.
Call utilities before cutoff — almost all have hardship programs.

articles.title: How Discounts Stack — 30% Off Plus 20% Off Is Not 50% Off
articles.subhead: The compounding math, and why stacked discounts look bigger than they are.

current.description: How stacked percentage discounts actually combine — the multiplication rule, common mistakes, and store examples.
current.outcomeLine: Multiply the "keep" fractions. 30% off + 20% off = 0.70 × 0.80 = 56% of price = 44% off.
current.intro: Stacked discounts do not add — they compound. Each discount applies to the already-discounted price, not the original. This is why "30% off plus another 20% off" is less than 50% off.
keyPoints: Formula: combined "keep" fraction = (1 − d1) × (1 − d2) × …
Order does not matter — the math is the same whichever discount applies first.
A flat-dollar discount after a percentage is NOT compounding — it subtracts.
Tax is usually applied AFTER discounts, so the tax amount goes down with discounts too.

examples.body: 0.50 × 0.90 = 0.45. Pay 45% of original, save 55% — not 60%.

examples.title: Flat + percentage
examples.body: £10 off then 20% off: 100 − 10 = 90, then 90 × 0.80 = £72. Order matters here; read store rules.

whenToUse.note: Main tool — handles single and stacked discounts, shows the math step by step.
For ad-hoc percentage operations outside the shopping context.

faq.q: What if the store lets me choose the order?
faq.a: The math is the same either way with two percentage discounts. Order only matters when you mix percentage and flat-dollar discounts.

articles.title: How to Compare Two Discounts — Percent Off vs. Dollar Off
articles.subhead: When is £20 off better than 20% off? Depends on the price.

current.description: How to decide whether a percentage discount or a flat dollar discount is better value, with worked examples.
current.outcomeLine: Convert both to final-price-in-pounds, then compare. Do not compare percentage to dollar-off directly.
current.intro: The question "is 20% off better than £30 off?" has no universal answer — it depends entirely on the price of the item. Always convert both options to the same unit (final price) before comparing.
keyPoints: Convert both discounts to final price, then compare. Never compare % to £ directly.
Cross-over point: for an X% discount vs. a £Y flat discount, they are equal when price = Y / (X/100).
Rule of thumb: percentage discounts win on expensive items; flat discounts win on cheap items.
£20 off vs. 20% off — equal at £100. Above £100, the percentage wins; below, the flat does.
Watch for minimum-spend thresholds on flat-dollar discounts — they may not apply to your cart.

examples.title: £20 off vs. 20% off, item £60
examples.body: Flat: £60 − £20 = £40. Percentage: £60 × 0.80 = £48. Flat wins by £8.

examples.title: £20 off vs. 20% off, item £200
examples.body: Flat: £200 − £20 = £180. Percentage: £200 × 0.80 = £160. Percentage wins by £20.

examples.title: Cross-over
examples.body: At £100, both land on £80. Below £100 the flat wins; above, the percentage.

whenToUse.note: Main tool — enter both discount options and compare final prices directly.
For running the cross-over math when comparing multiple offers.

faq.q: What about percentage off a sale price?
faq.a: That is a stacked discount — compound them. If the item is already 25% off and you have a 10% coupon, total discount = 1 − 0.75 × 0.90 = 32.5%, not 35%.

faq.q: Are loyalty discounts applied before or after coupons?
faq.a: Varies by store. Check the sequence at checkout — sometimes you get a bigger discount by applying the percentage first and the flat after, or vice versa.

articles.title: When to Tip and How Much — A Short Practical Guide
articles.h1: When to Tip and How Much
articles.subhead: The short version: US vs. UK/EU, services beyond restaurants, and split-bill math.

current.description: Practical tipping guide — US, UK, Europe, Asia — with service-by-service defaults and split-bill examples.
current.outcomeLine: Tip norms vary wildly by country and service — knowing the local default saves awkward math.
current.intro: Tipping is one of those everyday calculations where the rules differ by country, service, and sometimes by city. The mental-math is easy once you know the expected percentage.
keyPoints: US: 18–20% is standard at sit-down restaurants. 15% is low. 10% in some countries is normal.
UK / EU: 10–12.5% or a "service charge already added" line — check the bill first.
Rideshare (Uber, Lyft): 10–15% in the US; typically none in UK/EU unless you want to round up.
Hairdresser, barber, massage: 15–20% in the US; rounding up is common elsewhere.
Split bills: calculate tip on the pre-split total, then divide — not the other way around.

examples.title: US restaurant, $80 bill
examples.body: 20% tip = $16. Total $96. For 18%, subtract about 10% of the tip ($1.60).

examples.title: UK restaurant, £65 bill
examples.body: Check for service charge first. If none, 10% = £6.50 is a normal tip. 12.5% = £8.13.

examples.title: Split 4 ways
examples.body: $100 bill, 20% tip = $20, total $120. Divide by 4 = $30 each. Do NOT split first and tip per person — harder math, same answer.

whenToUse.note: Main tool — handles tip percentage and split-between-people in one step.
For edge cases (odd tip %, rounding-up questions).

faq.a: Common practice in the US: tip on pre-tax total. But many people tip on the total bill (including tax) because it is easier — the difference is usually a dollar or two.
In the US, 15% is the traditional "I'm signalling unhappy" tip; under 10% is a strong statement. Outside the US, reducing or removing the tip is more acceptable.

articles.title: Markup vs. Margin — The Difference That Trips Everyone Up
articles.subhead: Same two numbers, different denominator, different answer.

current.description: The difference between markup and margin in pricing, with formulas, examples, and the common mistake.
current.outcomeLine: Markup = profit ÷ cost. Margin = profit ÷ price. They are not the same.
current.intro: Markup and margin use the same profit number but different denominators — cost for markup, selling price for margin. This makes a 50% markup only a 33% margin, and most pricing errors come from confusing them.
keyPoints: Markup = (price − cost) ÷ cost. Denominator is COST.
Margin = (price − cost) ÷ price. Denominator is PRICE.
For a product that costs £10 and sells for £15: markup = 50%, margin = 33.3%.
Markup > margin always (for positive profits).
Retail uses margin; wholesale and trade often use markup. Know which a conversation is using.

examples.title: Target 40% margin
examples.body: Price = cost ÷ (1 − 0.40) = cost ÷ 0.60. A £30 item needs £50 retail.

whenToUse.note: The dedicated tool — enter cost and either markup % or margin %, get the other plus selling price.
For ad-hoc markup/margin checks when you already know the cost and price.

faq.q: Why does the same product have a 50% markup but only 33% margin?
faq.a: Markup uses cost (£10) as denominator: £5 profit ÷ £10 cost = 50%. Margin uses price (£15): £5 ÷ £15 = 33.3%. Same profit, different comparison basis.

faq.q: Which one should I quote to a customer?
faq.a: Neither — quote the price. Markup and margin are internal business metrics. Customers only care about what they pay.

articles.title: Break-Even Analysis Basics — When Does the Business Make Money?
articles.subhead: Fixed costs, variable costs, contribution margin — in the order you actually need them.

current.description: Plain-English break-even analysis — the formula, the components, and how to use it for pricing decisions.
current.outcomeLine: Break-even units = fixed costs ÷ contribution margin per unit. Everything else is detail.
current.intro: Break-even tells you the minimum number of units you need to sell to cover your costs. Past that point, every unit is profit. The calculation is one formula and three numbers.
keyPoints: Fixed costs: expenses that do not scale with units sold (rent, salaries, equipment).
Variable cost per unit: what each unit costs to produce (materials, shipping, processing).
Contribution margin per unit = price − variable cost. This is what each sale contributes to covering fixed costs.
Break-even units = fixed costs ÷ contribution margin per unit.
Break-even revenue = break-even units × price.

examples.title: Small product
examples.body: Fixed £5,000/month, variable £3/unit, price £13. Contribution = £10. Break-even = 500 units/month.

examples.title: Higher margin
examples.body: Same fixed £5,000, variable £5, price £25. Contribution = £20. Break-even = 250 units — half the volume.

examples.title: Price sensitivity
examples.body: A £2 price cut (13 → 11) on the small product changes contribution from £10 to £8. Break-even rises from 500 to 625 units.

whenToUse.note: Main tool — enter fixed, variable, price; get units and revenue.
For pairing break-even with margin targets when setting a price.
For sensitivity analysis — "what if costs rise 10%?"

faq.q: What if I have multiple products?
faq.a: Use a weighted average contribution margin across your product mix, or compute break-even per product line. Mixing the math only works if the mix stays stable.

faq.q: Should I include my own salary in fixed costs?
faq.a: If you are running it as a business (not a hobby), yes — otherwise break-even hides the cost of your time. For side projects this is optional.

rules: Each question must read like a real search query — not marketing-speak.
First sentence of answer is a complete standalone 15-25 word definition/answer (Google quotes it directly).
Full answer 40-60 words. Tight. Front-load the answer.
Cover distinct intents: definition, comparison, edge-case, how-to, troubleshooting. Not 3 rewordings of the same angle.
Include one target keyword in at least half the questions.
Add a faq_intent tag per entry: "definition" | "comparison" | "edge-case" | "how-to" | "troubleshooting" | "cost" | "trust"
faq_rules.target_count: 5-7 entries per article, 4-6 per tool page

structure: ## H2 section 1 — deeper "what is it" / definition with context
## H2 section 2 — the mechanic / how it works (with one small example)
## H2 section 3 — when to use vs when to avoid
## H2 section 4 — worked example or walkthrough
## H2 section 6 — tool-specific tips (link out to the tool)
longform_rules.tone: Concrete, useful, first-person-plural light. No filler. No "in conclusion". Short paragraphs.
links: Every time you mention a sibling tool in this cluster, link it with the exact URL from this brief (tools[].url).
Every time you mention a sibling article, link it with the exact URL from this brief (articles[].url).
Do not invent URLs or link to external competitors.
forbidden: Medical/legal/financial advice language ("you should", "we recommend") — use neutral framing.
Generic SEO boilerplate ("in today's fast-paced world").

internal_linking.rule: Use only URLs that appear in this JSON. Prefer linking to sibling tools/articles in THIS cluster over outbound links.

quality_gates: No fabricated facts — if uncertain, phrase as "in practice" or remove.
Every H2 section earns its space — cut if it restates the intro.
Zero AI-tells: "it is important to note", "in conclusion", "let us delve", "furthermore".
